title: Dyeing with chamomile yellow
subtitle: Extracting a clear, warm yellow from dried chamomile flowers on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Chamomile flowers (Anthemis tinctoria, dyer's chamomile, or Chamaemelum nobile) produce a clear yellow dye with good light-fastness on alum-mordanted wool. Dyer's chamomile gives the strongest, most saturated yellow; common chamomile produces a paler, slightly greener tone. This guide covers a simple single-bath extraction and dyeing method suitable for 100 g of pre-mordanted wool.
sourceNotes: Chamomile as a yellow dye source is documented in contemporary natural dyeing guides. Dyer's chamomile (Anthemis tinctoria) is the standard source for saturated yellow in European dye plant tradition. Method is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Potassium aluminium sulphate, the most common mordant in natural dyeing. Applied before dyeing, it improves dye uptake and light-fastness without significantly altering the dye colour. Standard quantity is 15-20 g per 100 g of dry fibre.

The point at which a dye bath has given most of its colour to the fibre. An exhausted bath is pale or near-clear and produces weaker results on subsequent fibre lots. A second batch dyed in an exhausted bath produces a lighter, more muted tone.

content.text: Chamomile yellow is produced from the flowers alone, not the stems or leaves. An
applied to the wool before dyeing is required for good take-up and light-fastness. After dyeing, the
level determines whether a second lighter batch is worth running.

items.name: Dried chamomile flowers (Anthemis tinctoria for strongest yellow)
Stainless steel or enamel dye pot (dedicated)
Fine straining bag or muslin cloth

content.text: Place the dried chamomile flowers in a muslin bag or straining bag and put them in the dye pot with enough cold water to cover the fibre generously. Bring slowly to the boil, then simmer for 30-40 minutes. Remove the flower bag and squeeze it gently to extract the remaining dye liquid. The bath will be a golden amber colour.

Allow the bath to cool slightly to around 60 degrees Celsius, then add the pre-wetted, alum-mordanted wool. Raise the temperature slowly to 80-85 degrees Celsius over 20-30 minutes. This slow rise prevents wool from felting and allows even dye uptake. Hold at 80-85 degrees for 45 minutes to an hour, stirring gently every 10 minutes.

Turn off the heat and allow the wool to cool in the dye bath for 20-30 minutes. This additional time in the cooling bath deepens the colour slightly. Remove the wool, squeeze gently without wringing, and rinse in water of the same temperature. Rinse until the water runs clear, then dry in the shade.
Step 4: Run a second lighter batch (optional)

If the bath still has colour, add a second pre-wetted mordanted skein and repeat the heating sequence. The second batch will be a paler, cooler yellow. This is a useful way to produce a tonal range from a single dye session. Once the bath is fully exhausted and nearly clear, dispose of it by diluting and pouring down the drain: chamomile dye bath is non-toxic and safe for disposal.

items.problem: The yellow is dull or greenish rather than clear and warm
items.cause: Common chamomile used instead of dyer's chamomile, or iron contamination in the dye pot
items.fix: Use Anthemis tinctoria (dyer's chamomile) for the clearest, warmest yellow. Ensure the dye pot is free of iron deposits that could sadden the colour.

items.problem: The yellow has faded noticeably after a few weeks
items.cause: Wool not mordanted before dyeing, or mordant amount insufficient
items.fix: Chamomile yellow requires a full alum pre-mordant at 15-20 g per 100 g fibre. Without mordanting, the colour will have poor light-fastness and wash-fastness.

items.problem: The wool has felted during dyeing
items.cause: Temperature raised too quickly, or bath agitated too vigorously
items.fix: Raise temperature at no more than 5 degrees Celsius per minute. Stir gently with a slow, lifting motion rather than stirring rapidly.
